He had a little sister born and his mother passed away and so the girls were really to the older girls were really too young to take care of two little children like that so they decided to send those two to the orphans home and they called the orphans home and the man came to get these two children and he was driving a team and here he was holding a baby and a little boy sitting beside him and all at once the little boy was missing. Well he didn't dare leave the team and he had a baby he couldn't run very far so he had to wait till he got the baby to the orphans home before he could give a notice and and they'd go look for the little boy and by that time my father had escaped far enough that they didn't find him and he told us afterwards that he hid under porches and behind garbage cans and in barns and under hay and words: well this this living alone having to live alone when the husband was out logging camps i've known of this to be the case and a lot of oh yes yes that that was one of the things you know when you lived up there in the mountains like he was supposed to go and work a logging camp and yet it seems like mostly it's the men that i've talked to who were out of the camps and not the wives who were who were at home and it sounds like it would be pretty rough to it's terrible i'm telling you especially when the children's fall after they get a little bigger than it is it's a bad that anyhow the and the amount of work that there is there's hogs to feed and cows to feed and milk and the horses to take care of of course i only kept up one team most of the time the ones that i could use fall water and the rest stayed in the pasture and then sometimes you know when them gets lame or something you have to go get another one that's it all takes time and i had a garden and canning to do and i can remember many times that i sat up and tried to keep the fire going and the canner boiling so it's about two o 'clock in the morning it takes a while to snap enough beans my canner had held seven quarts and then get the beans cooked and they had to be cooked four hours and i was always glad when i canned fruit because that went quickly you know but anyway it all paid off my kids are all healthy and happy i hope and they're sure good to me how often would he get to come home oh he's always home he came home Saturday night and stayed the Sunday night did you get to see neighbors very much when he was away well only if they came to visit you know they several of them would make it a point to come around and see if we were okay especially Mr. Swenson my son of uh what was the one you had on the tape Ed Ed Ed's son

words: Eddall Mr. and Mrs. Swenson were wonderful i'll tell you that couldn't be better neighbors they used to come by and stop and and you know see how we were and maybe they'd bring a fresh piece of venison or one time i know they brought a piece of bear meat that was the first bear meat i had ever eaten and i've heard so many things but anyhow i put it in the oven and roasted it and fixed it like i usually did the roast and it was the best meat i think i ever ate i don't know whether i was so hungry or whether it actually was that good but anyhow i never will forget that but they were just real good parents our neighbors uh

Inger hauled but she had a truck her husband worked too they all did and but she'd only get one barrel usually oh yes then there was a pond that we used to have to go and dip out of that wasn't any fun either well you were dipping it then yeah you just had a pail that you yeah just a pail and at the upper place not at the well and that's where my youngest daughter lives now is where we used to dip the water i wonder how much a barrel a barrel would hold 52 gallons and you had how many four of them did you have to be careful to conserve that yes and we had one big white face cow we've watered in the tub you know put a hose in it you know and sucked on it and held it in the tub and this big white face cow she'd stand there and drink and drink and drink and pretty soon she couldn't drink but she'd hold her mouth in the water and she was so big around oh dear if she'd walk she'd have rattled and if anything else would come up you know and then come on that side and i used to get a stick i just loved her but i had to get a stick anyhow and make her get out of there you know she'd look at you just like what'd you do that for oh dear but you know that she knew that next day she might not get a drink you know went over there and somebody got a hit at me why then they didn't get any water well how long did it take for the water to come back once it was pumped dry usually about 12 hours or 14 hours in the well this other place up above there it ran a little stream most the time and we just dug out you know with a shovel and made a place where you could dip with the bucket and of course the pond you had to put a board in it so you'd have a solid place to step but anyway how many families do you think were drawing water that way every day how many families do you think oh well there was
